text.title: TDD Red-Green-Refactor Cycle
text.summary: Per-behavior cycle rules, phase-locked prompt patterns, and the triangulation requirement that prevents hardcoded returns.

rule.statement: Run one behavior per cycle. Do not write multiple failing tests before implementing. Do not implement before seeing the test fail.
rule.rationale: Running multiple tests before implementing hides which test drives which code. The cycle only works when each test forces exactly one implementation decision.

rule.statement: Verify RED before proceeding to GREEN. The orchestrating agent must confirm the test runner reports the new test as failing before allowing any implementation writes.
rule.rationale: The skip-RED failure mode is the most common way agents defeat TDD: they write implementation first, then write tests that pass trivially against it.

rule.statement: Triangulate: write 2-3 input cases that force the implementation to generalize. A single test case tempts the agent to return a hardcoded value.
rule.rationale: An agent that can satisfy a test for input=5 by returning 5 will do exactly that. The second test case for a different input forces a general solution.

rule.statement: Refactor step is mandatory. Run the full suite after every refactor to confirm no regressions. Make refactoring a separate agent invocation with the explicit goal "do not add features."

example.note: RED phase prompt: "Output ONLY a new test in [test file]. Do not modify implementation files. Run pytest [path::test] -x. Expected: 1 failed, 0 passed."

GREEN phase prompt: "Implement minimum code to make test [name] pass. Do not change other tests. Do not add features not exercised by the failing test. Run pytest [path::test] -x. Expected: 1 passed."
REFACTOR phase prompt: "Improve [file] without changing public API or test outcomes. Run full suite pytest -q. Expected: same pass count as before. Block if any test now fails."

rule.rationale: Descriptive names make test failure output immediately actionable without reading the test body.
